text: Welcome to the DaVinci Simulator training video series. This is the first video of a series of videos through which we hope to develop independent training. I wanted to start off with an overview of the surgical console as well as how to navigate the menu of the DaVinci Skills Simulator software itself. As you'll see in all my videos, the screen is going to be divided into three partitions. The largest partition is actually the simulator task. But the other two additions are webcams that are mounted on the console itself to project the foot pedal, and the controller arms. I believe for independent training, you have to see all three of these.

The instructor demonstrates the ideal way to hold the DaVinci simulator controllers, using the middle and thumb fingers while the index finger controls the clutch. The foot pedal operation is shown, focusing on the camera and clutch pedals.

As for ergonomics, that can be easily adjusted with the pad or the controls on the left hand side of the armrest. Those control the visor as well as the armrest and also the foot pedal, which comes closer and further away from you as you can see in the video there. So to log in, you'd use your right arm as a mouse. And to click, you just approximate your fingers together. That takes you to the login menu. You use the taskbar on the right to scroll down. For the surgery group, your login starts with SUR and then your first name, last initial. You click onto that. All your passwords are four zeros. And then you press the OK button in that. logs you in. To log out, you use the log out of the upper right side of the screen. So to demonstrate this, I'm going to use the default password. And then after I log in, it gives you an overview of all the broad categories in which all tasks are available. And they usually divide it into what they're going to teach you. To get back, you use the main menu to the up left corner of the screen.

There's an important surgeon console overview, and that actually goes into explicit details about using the simulator, the ergonomics, and the overview of the controls. So if you're interested, you can definitely go into that. I wanted to start off in the playground which is under the games category. Just to give you a brief overview of the console again. Basically your arms, your forearms have to be always on that armrest so you have to adjust the ergonomics accordingly. We also talked about using your middle and your thumb in the controls leaving your index finger for the clutch. Now a lot of you push your fingers all the way in. All you need to do is just put the tips of your fingers. That allows you to use the ender wrist a lot more efficiently. To grasp anything, you just approximate your fingers together. Now, one of the great features of the robot is the ender wrist. That gives you a wide range of motion. But also something that's very important, it depends on how you hold objects. So if you hold that ring perpendicular, that would give you a wider range of motion versus holding it from the side. That would definitely limit your range of motion.

Another thing I wanted to stress on is the clutch. There's the finger clutch and there's the foot clutch. The foot clutch is above the camera pedal where my foot is in the video down to the left and when you press the clutch you'll actually see something appearing in the screen right in the middle around six o'clock. So as you can see here when I press the clutch I can actually move my hand in the screen and that does not reflect a movement in the robot itself. It's to adjust your ergonomics. If you use the finger clutch you can control each arm individually however the foot clutch will control both arms together. Another feature which might help you follow through with the split screen videos is that in the same position where the clutch icon appeared, there's also a camera icon that appears when you operate the camera pedal. I've also preserved the sound so you can hear me operating the camera pedal rather than actually looking at the screen. Moving the camera is just like holding a newspaper. You bring it closer to you using your arms while operating the foot pedal. You take it to the side so you reach out with your arms and then you move the camera accordingly. You can also rotate it by rotating your arms and then again there's a feature at the twelve o'clock position in the screen that shows you your horizon. You won't notice that you're off horizon in the beginning when you're training. You're going to have to keep an eye on that icon at twelve o'clock to actually show you if you're at horizon or not.

You can also do a dual movement where you twist and approximate or twist and push out at the same time. Again, that's something that advanced users would use, but I would encourage you to try it while using it. Now when you're operating with the camera you have to continue to keep your foot on the camera pedal while using the camera. As soon as it comes off you'll be operating the robotic arms. The last thing I want to touch on is the fourth arm. So there's a side pedal there that you tap with the side of your foot and that operates the fourth arm or the retraction arm. It toggles with one of the arms and that depends on which side the arm is. If I operate one of the arms then another arm is going to be static and I move two arms at the same time. It's usually a retraction arm, so you'd use it to retract something and then toggle the other operating or working arm back into motion and start using both of them together. You also have to realize that when you toggle back, you're going to have to use the clutch button because your arm is hanging up there and your static arm was in a different position. So you have to clutch first and then start moving your arm.

Finally, when toggling the fourth arm back, if it's holding something, you have to maintain both fingers approximated because when you let go or toggle back into your fourth arm, it actually lets go. So it's demonstrated here in the ring. I'm using my fourth arm and then toggling back to my working arm. And then when I toggle back into the fourth arm, if I don't keep my fingers approximated, whatever I'm holding there is going to drop. In that case to reactivate your fourth arm you actually use the ender wrist and twist it left and right. Finally, holding a needle with the robot is a little bit different than when you do it laparoscopically. You hold a needle at the same angle. The only thing you have to realize is that you have an extra wrist there. It's not a straight stick instrument like lab. So you have to bring that into account. I usually hold a needle at a forty-five degree angle. That decreases the profile of the wrist, especially when we're doing anastomosis deep in the pelvis or in a confined area. While if you take it directly, you know, perpendicular to the needle itself, that profile might hit the pelvic bone, for example, when we're doing radical prostatectomies in urology, so we tend to keep it at a forty-five degree angle.

If you have a lot of space, then definitely take it at ninety degrees, but just be cognizant of the fact that when you're moving the needle here, you're going to have an extra elbow or wrist that's going to be moving with you. You know, based on my experience with laparoscopy and robotics, in the beginning you feel a little bit awkward doing suturing with the robotics, but then after a while you really realize that this is very easy to do with the robot because of the ender wrist and the extra range of motion that you have. Transferring the needle is, again, the same way that you would do it laparoscopically. You're using the same laparoscopic principles. Generally, this is just another tool for minimum invasive surgery. It takes a little bit of getting used to, so don't get discouraged in the beginning if you feel that you were better at lab than you were at robotics. After a while, you will definitely feel that this is a great tool and something that will help you take your skills to the next level.
